description: In particular, this sparrow's facial pattern (eye ring and orangish lores) as well as its underside (buffy and clean except for a breast band of narrow streaks) led me to this ID.I noticed this bird perched a few feet off the ground in a shrub at the edge of the Nature Park fields. It was clearly an interesting sparrow. This was during a sort of fallout after last September's big cold front, and the park was filled with birds, including sparrows.This bird immediately struck me as an Ammodramus sparrow and probably a Grasshopper Sparrow — different than the many other sparrows present. It was warm brown overall with a distinct eyering and, most notably, some yellow near the lores. The bird did not vocalize and sat perched in the bush before flying off to the east, following some other sparrows. It seemed to be associating loosely with the myriad other sparrows present that day.
